title: Effects of Resistance Training Frequency on Measures of Muscle Hypertrophy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
population: Healthy adults in experimental resistance training studies
keyFinding: When weekly training volume is equated, training a muscle group about twice per week appears to produce better hypertrophy than once per week.
takeaway: If muscle growth is the goal, hitting a muscle more than once per week is usually a strong default.

title: Strength and Hypertrophy Adaptations Between Low- vs. High-Load Resistance Training: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
population: Adults in resistance training studies comparing low and high loads
keyFinding: Muscle hypertrophy was similar between low-load and high-load training protocols when sets were performed to momentary muscular failure, while heavy loads improved 1RM strength more.
takeaway: You do not need heavy loads for hypertrophy every time, but heavier work still matters if maximal strength is also a goal.

title: Influence of Resistance Training Proximity-to-Failure on Skeletal Muscle Hypertrophy: A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis
population: Healthy adults of varying ages and resistance training experience
keyFinding: The review found no strong evidence that training to momentary muscular failure is superior to non-failure training for hypertrophy.
takeaway: Sets probably need to be hard, but every set does not need to end in absolute failure to support muscle growth.

title: Effects of range of motion on muscle development during resistance training interventions: A systematic review
authors: Brad J Schoenfeld and Jozo Grgic
population: Adults in longitudinal resistance training studies
keyFinding: Full range of motion appeared to benefit lower-body hypertrophy compared with partial range of motion, while upper-body findings were more mixed.
takeaway: Using a full range of motion is a practical default, especially for lower-body exercises.

title: Resistance Training Volume Enhances Muscle Hypertrophy but Not Strength in Trained Men
keyFinding: Men performing higher training volumes increased muscle hypertrophy more than lower-volume groups, while strength gains were not clearly greater.
takeaway: Doing more hard sets can help hypertrophy in trained lifters, but adding volume has a clearer effect on size than on strength.

title: Resistance training prescription for muscle strength and hypertrophy in healthy adults: a systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis
type: Systematic review and Bayesian network meta-analysis
population: Healthy adults across randomized resistance training trials
keyFinding: All resistance training prescriptions outperformed non-exercise controls for hypertrophy, and higher-load multiset twice-weekly training ranked highest for hypertrophy.
takeaway: Hypertrophy can be built with many reasonable programs, but multiset training is a strong common denominator.

title: Effect of free-weight vs. machine-based strength training on maximal strength, hypertrophy and jump performance - a systematic review and meta-analysis
population: Adults aged 18 to 60 in studies comparing free weights and machines
keyFinding: No clear difference was found between free-weight and machine-based training for hypertrophy in direct comparisons.
takeaway: Exercise selection can be based on goals, comfort, and access because both free weights and machines can support muscle growth.
